text: The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety and places the first clip proximally on the cystic duct before creating a cystotomy for trans-cystic common bile duct exploration.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to open the cystic duct, navigating around visible valves within the duct.
The surgeon uses a Cook CBD Exploration Kit, inserting a needle followed by a J-wire into the cystic duct for exploration, then removes the needle while leaving the wire in place.
The surgeon navigates between the ribs in a morbidly obese patient, inserting a dilator and sheath. The dilator is removed, leaving the sheath in place.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers a 150-centimeter soft wire, ensuring not to contact the duodenum while orienting towards the abdominal wall.
The surgeon inserts a cholangiogram catheter into the cystic duct and common bile duct, flushing these structures. The procedure follows the administration of glucagon by anesthesia.
The surgeon inserts a 150-centimeter soft wire into the surgical field, using visual cues for guidance without x-ray assistance. A balloon is prepared for subsequent insertion.
The surgeon introduces a balloon catheter to dilate the cystic duct to eight millimeters, following the previous measurement of the common bile duct at approximately 1.2 centimeters.
The surgeon dilates the cystic duct to eight millimeters, ensuring it does not exceed the size of the common bile duct. The dilation is maintained for three minutes.
The surgeon removes the balloon from the dilated cystic duct using the Seldinger technique, maneuvering over the previously inserted soft wire.

The surgeon introduces a ureteroscope into the surgical field, utilizing a video-in-video or tile pro setup for enhanced visualization. Water is continuously flushed through the cystoscope to maintain a clear view as the stones are removed from the duct.
The surgeon uses a disposable ureteroscope, grasping and pushing it into position to facilitate the procedure's progress.
The surgeon observes a stone larger than the dilated cystic duct and extends the cystotomy longitudinally, occasionally approaching the area directly above the common bile duct.

The surgeon gently manipulates the cystic duct opening to facilitate the removal of a stone, which pops out successfully. The stone extraction is performed without suturing, and the surgeon mentions the option of lithotripsy as an alternative technique.

The feasibility of doing trans-cystic CBD exploration depends on how many stones you have, the cystic duct insertion, whether it's a low cystic duct insertion or high cystic duct insertion, and also how big the stones are. If the stones are more than eight millimeter, forget it. It's better just to open the column bile duct or break the stones down using the lithotripsy. You can either use laser lithotripsy or hydro lithotripsy.

The surgeon uses a ureteroscope to grasp and extract what was initially thought to be the last stone from the bile duct. A completion cholangiogram is performed to confirm the absence of remaining stones.

The surgeon uses a ureteroscope to grab and remove a stone from the common bile duct. The common bile duct is then examined to ensure no additional stones are present. The ureteroscope is advanced towards the duodenum for further inspection.

The surgeon visualizes the duodenum and examines it slowly, similar to a colonoscopy, while retracting. Despite initial comfort with the appearance of the structures, a cholangiogram is performed, revealing an abnormality with no flow.
The surgeon observes the meniscus sign, indicating no filling defect in the common bile duct, but notes the absence of flow into the duodenum during the cholangiogram.
The surgeon ensures water flow through the choledochoscope for clear visualization. A stone is identified above the cystic duct insertion, which was previously missed during examination.
The surgeon observes the thickness of the gastrographin during the cholangiogram, identifying the final stone. After removing the stone, the cholangiogram shows free flow into the duodenum.

The surgeon performs a cholangiogram, visualizing the proximal biliary tree, including the hepatic common bile duct, and confirms free flow into the duodenum, indicating successful completion of the procedure.

Another point to make is though this procedure is very easy to perform, you got to know who is a good candidate. So if you have five stones or less, they're eight millimeter or less in size, and your cystic duct is not lowly inserted into the Common hepatic duct, this is a very doable procedure. Otherwise you might have to use either lithotripsy or open the common bile duct or do an ERCP. I think all general surgeons should be cleaning their stones themselves. It's a very fun and nice procedure to teach.
